title: IVF + PQ Combined
concept: Using coarse IVF partitioning for candidate selection and PQ compression for memory-efficient scoring.

exercises.prompt: How does Product Quantization (PQ) achieve high compression method from the last lesson) compress high-level prior lesson) reduce vector memory footprints?

options.text: By dropping half of the dimensions randomly.
By splitting vectors into sub-vectors and quantizing each chunk into a codebook index.
By converting all floating-point numbers into 32-bit integers.

exercises.explain: Product Quantization (PQ) splits a high-dimensional vector into several smaller sub-vectors (chunks) and quantizes each sub-vector independently against a codebook.

cards.title: The Ultimate Vector Duo
cards.body: Individually, IVF speeds up searches by skipping most of the database, and PQ saves memory by compressing vectors. What happens when we stack them together? We get lightning-fast vector search that fits on a single affordable server.

cards.title: IVF + PQ Synergy
cards.body: Combining IVF and PQ gives you the best of both worlds. IVF groups vectors into clusters so you only search nearby groups, while PQ compresses the vectors inside those clusters to save massive amounts of RAM and speed up distance calculation.

cards.title: The Hybrid Library Assistant
cards.body: Imagine a massive multi-story library. IVF is the directory that tells you which specific shelf has books on your topic. PQ is a speed-reading method that lets you read a summary of each book on that shelf in seconds instead of reading every word.

cards.title: Scaling to Millions of Images
cards.body: For an image search engine with 100 million vectors, IVF narrows the search down from 100 million to 100,000 candidate vectors. Then, PQ computes fast approximate distances on just those 100,000 compressed vectors in milliseconds.

cards.title: Is it Lossless?
cards.body: Actually, combining IVF and PQ does introduce a small double-penalty to accuracy (recall) because both clustering and quantization introduce approximations. However, the enormous savings in memory and speed usually make this trade-off highly worthwhile.

cards.title: Division of Labor
cards.body: Compare how IVF limits WHERE we look, while PQ limits HOW MUCH memory each item takes up during comparison.

left.text: Filters the database down to the most relevant clusters of vectors.

right.text: Compresses the vectors in those clusters to make distance scoring ultra-fast.

exercises.prompt: How do IVF and PQ divide the work when combined in a vector database index?

options.text: IVF compresses the vectors and PQ clusters them into groups.
IVF performs coarse partitioning for candidate selection, and PQ compresses the vectors for low-memory scoring.
IVF guarantees 100% exact accuracy while PQ handles the speed boost.
IVF and PQ cannot be used together because they perform the same step.

exercises.explain: IVF reduces the search space by selecting only relevant clusters, and PQ compresses the vector representations within those clusters to reduce memory consumption.

exercises.prompt: True or False: Using IVF + PQ allows you to store larger vector datasets in RAM compared to using IVF alone with uncompressed raw vectors.
exercises.explain: Yes, because PQ compresses the vector data, the combined index occupies significantly less memory (RAM) than storing raw vectors.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing phrase to describe the primary role of IVF in the combined pipeline.
exercises.sentence: In a combined index, IVF is responsible for candidate selection by filtering out unrelated clusters.
exercises.explain: IVF does the heavy lifting of pruning the search space (candidate selection) so that the PQ engine only has to score a tiny fraction of the database.

exercises.prompt: Which of the following describes how IVF and PQ work together to execute a query?

options.text: Because IVF stores vectors as full raw floats, PQ is used to compress the centroids themselves rather than the vectors inside the clusters.
By first using IVF to find the nearest cluster centroids, we only load and compute the PQ codes for a small subset of the database, saving both memory and CPU cycles.
The query vector is converted to a PQ code first, which is then used to find the IVF centroid using exact matrix multiplication.

exercises.explain: Option B uses natural conversational language to describe the combined IVF-PQ pipeline. Options A and C are typical human-written distractors with incorrect mechanics.

exercises.prompt: When a user query is sent to a combined IVF-PQ index, how do the two algorithms coordinate to return the top results?

options.text: PQ codes are calculated first to group all vectors, and then IVF finds the centroids
IVF narrows the search to a few candidate clusters, and PQ scores the items in those clusters
Both algorithms run independently and their scores are averaged at the end
IVF compresses the vectors into binary codes, and PQ partitions them into rooms

exercises.explain: In an IVF-PQ index, the search is two-fold: first, the query's IVF centroid list is used to find the closest clusters (coarse selection). Then, only the vectors in those clusters are scored using the PQ codes (fine-grained compressed distance calculation). This avoids evaluating the PQ codes of all vectors in the database.
